title: Animal Welfare and Economic Prosperity
keyFinding: Humane farming practices drive access to premium export markets, reduce costs through healthier animals, and boost farm productivity — making animal welfare a strategic economic investment, not just a moral one.
impactSummary: EU pork producers meeting higher welfare standards gained access to premium markets in Japan and South Korea. Farmers in Germany and the Netherlands who adopted welfare-friendly practices received retailer premiums and saw market transformation. The report frames animal welfare not as a cost center but as a strategic business investment that strengthens long-term farm resilience and profitability.

title: Animal Welfare and Farm Economy: Exploring the Relationship Between Dairy Animal Welfare Indicators and Economic Efficiency
keyFinding: Improving animal health on dairy farms generates multiple simultaneous economic benefits — reduced mortality costs, better product quality, improved farmer job satisfaction, and stronger corporate social responsibility standing.
impactSummary: This study is one of the first to embed actual animal welfare indicators into farm efficiency analysis. The finding that matters most for CAWE's audience: welfare and profitability are not opposites. Farms that invest in animal health measurably outperform those that do not across multiple economic dimensions simultaneously.

title: Animal Stress and Welfare During Transport and Slaughtering: An Outline for Future Policies
keyFinding: More than 20 million farm animals die during road transport in the US each year before reaching slaughter — representing direct, measurable economic losses to producers in addition to profound welfare failures.
impactSummary: Every year in the United States, more than 20 million farm animals — chickens, pigs, and cattle — die during road transport before they ever reach a slaughterhouse. These are not just welfare failures; they are direct economic losses to producers. This peer-reviewed study documents the scale of transport mortality and the policy levers that could reduce it, including shorter transport times, better vehicle design, and mobile slaughter alternatives. The economic case for reform is built into the mortality numbers themselves.

title: A Scoping Review of Incentives and Disincentives for Animal Welfare-Improving Farming Practices
keyFinding: Outdoor access and product quality are clear economic incentives for welfare adoption — farms offering outdoor access can charge price premiums consumers recognize and trust. Rigid regulatory standards applied uniformly often increase costs without allowing flexible farm responses.
impactSummary: Why don't more farmers adopt humane practices even when evidence suggests they improve profitability? This study analyzes 74 published papers to identify the economic incentives and barriers. The policy implication is clear: voluntary incentive programs may outperform mandates for driving welfare improvements, because they allow farms to respond to market signals rather than compliance costs.

title: Are They Buying It? US Consumers' Changing Attitudes Toward More Humanely Raised Meat, Eggs, and Dairy
keyFinding: US consumers are willing to pay a 32% premium for humanely certified eggs and a 48% premium for certified chicken. 78% require independent third-party verification — industry self-certification alone does not move them.
impactSummary: US consumers say they want humane products — and this peer-reviewed study of 1,000 US consumers confirms they will pay more. More than half said they would choose a restaurant specifically because it served welfare-certified products and would pay $5 or more extra per entrée. The market signal is clear: credible third-party certification unlocks significant consumer willingness to pay, creating a direct economic incentive for producers to adopt higher welfare standards.

title: State Policies for Farm Animal Welfare in Production Practices
keyFinding: Since 2002, 14 US states have passed laws targeting farm animal welfare — primarily banning confinement practices. California's Proposition 12 demonstrates how state policy can reach across state lines to affect producers nationwide.
impactSummary: State farm animal welfare laws have created a patchwork of protections across the US. California's Proposition 12 — which bans sale of pork from gestation-crate operations regardless of where the pig was raised — is the highest-profile example of how state policy now shapes national markets. This USDA report maps the full landscape of what those policies cover and how retail sales restrictions in one state can drive industry-wide change.

title: Increasing Protections for Farm Animals
keyFinding: Federal law currently provides almost no protection for farm animals on the farm. The Animal Welfare Act explicitly excludes them. Industry opposition to reform centers on one economic argument: that higher welfare standards will reduce supply and raise consumer prices.
impactSummary: For 10 billion animals raised for food in the US each year, the law simply does not apply on the farm. This 2024 policy analysis argues for a federal standard with USDA enforcement authority. The central economic debate is laid bare: will higher welfare standards raise food prices? This article puts that tradeoff in plain view and lets the evidence speak — making it an ideal entry point for policymakers and general audiences alike.

title: Veterinarians Report Increasing Price Sensitivity, Decreasing Visits
keyFinding: 81% of surveyed veterinarians reported clients were more cost-sensitive than in 2024, when the figure was 72%. Clients are increasingly declining recommended care, starting with diagnostics, then nonessential procedures and preventive care.
impactSummary: Economic pressure is now visibly changing the actual medical care animals receive. Preventive care is the first casualty — clients delay or skip routine visits when prices rise, creating downstream health costs that far exceed what prevention would have cost. This is not just a pet-owner problem; veterinary practices themselves are feeling the squeeze, with profitability improving for only 32% of respondents in 2025, the lowest level in several years.

title: 2025 Pet Lifetime of Care Study
keyFinding: 80% of pet owners underestimate lifetime pet care expenses. Dog care costs over 15 years now range from 2,125 to 0,602 — up 11.65% since 2022. Cat care costs surged 19.4% to 0,073–7,106. Nearly 50% of pet owners worry about unexpected veterinary bills, up from 33% in 2022.
impactSummary: Only 20% of pet owners have dedicated savings or insurance for pet emergencies, yet lifetime costs have risen sharply. This gap between expectation and reality is a structural economic problem, not just an individual budgeting failure. It directly predicts the care-deferral and shelter-surrender outcomes documented elsewhere in this repository. When owners cannot afford care, animals suffer — and the costs ultimately shift to shelters, municipalities, and taxpayers.

title: Disease Burden in Four Populations of Dog and Cat Breeds Compared to Mixed-Breed Dogs and European Shorthair Cats
keyFinding: Purebred dogs and cats carry a measurably higher disease burden than mixed-breed animals due to reduced genetic variation from inbreeding and repeated use of the same breeding stock, which leads to a greater incidence of inherited diseases.
impactSummary: For prospective pet owners, a purebred animal means higher lifetime veterinary costs — an economic consequence of breeding decisions made generations earlier. This study quantifies what many owners learn too late: the purchase price of a purebred pet is only the beginning. Inherited conditions including heart disease, hip dysplasia, and respiratory disorders generate ongoing costs that mixed-breed owners are statistically less likely to face.

title: Ten Inherited Disorders in Purebred Dogs by Functional Breed Groupings
authors: Bellumori TP, Famula TR, Bannasch DL, Belanger JM, Oberbauer AM
keyFinding: Analysis of 88,635 dogs identified ten inherited conditions with significantly greater prevalence in purebred dogs compared to mixed-breed dogs, including epilepsy, heart disease, elbow dysplasia, and intervertebral disk disease.
impactSummary: Each of the ten conditions identified carries significant, predictable veterinary costs over an animal's lifetime. This research gives consumers, breeders, and policymakers concrete breed-level data to connect purchasing decisions directly to downstream economic and welfare outcomes. A buyer who chooses a breed predisposed to epilepsy or heart disease is unknowingly committing to years of specialist care — costs that often lead to financial strain, deferred treatment, or ultimately surrender to a shelter.

title: 2025 Annual Data Report
keyFinding: 757,000 dogs and cats experienced a non-live outcome in 2025. 4.2 million dogs and cats were adopted, a 1% increase from 2024. Stray animals made up 59% of all intakes nationwide, with owner surrenders rising slightly to 30%.
impactSummary: This is the most current, authoritative national dataset on shelter outcomes in the US — essential baseline data for any economic analysis of sheltering costs and policy interventions. The slight rise in owner surrenders to 30% of intakes signals a worsening economic squeeze on pet owners. When households cannot afford veterinary care, food, or pet-friendly housing, animals enter the shelter system — shifting costs from private households to public and nonprofit infrastructure.

title: Examining the Relationship Between Social Vulnerability and Animal Shelter Intakes and Outcomes
keyFinding: Animals tended to enter shelters from areas of higher social vulnerability while being adopted out to homes across all vulnerability levels. Racial/ethnic minority status and socioeconomic status produced the strongest differences between intake and adoption vulnerability levels.
impactSummary: This peer-reviewed study provides hard data connecting community economic conditions directly to shelter intake patterns. Animals most at risk live in the same communities as the people most financially stressed — creating compounding harm. Shelter outcomes are not just an animal welfare problem; they are an economic equity problem. Interventions that address community economic stress — access to low-cost veterinary care, pet-friendly housing — are among the most effective tools for reducing shelter intakes.

title: Companion Animal Foster Caregiving: A Scoping Review
keyFinding: Foster care is an effective tool for improving animal welfare by removing animals from shelter stressors, providing greater social interaction, and offering opportunities for improved health and behavior outcomes.
impactSummary: Foster programs represent a lower-cost, higher-welfare alternative to traditional shelter housing. Animals in foster care show better health and behavioral outcomes than those housed in shelters — meaning the investment in foster program infrastructure pays returns in both animal welfare and adoption success rates. For CAWE's audience, this is a concrete example of where the humane choice and the economically efficient choice are the same choice.

title: Economic Costs of Biological Invasions Within North America
authors: Crystal-Ornelas R, Hudgins EJ, Cuthbert RN, et al.
keyFinding: Invasive species have cost the North American economy at least $1.26 trillion between 1960 and 2017, with annual costs climbing from $2 billion per year in the early 1960s to over $26 billion per year in the 2010s.
impactSummary: Invasive vertebrates were associated with the greatest costs, with agriculture and forestry sectors absorbing $527 billion cumulatively over six decades. These numbers reframe invasive species management not as an environmental luxury but as an economic imperative with measurable returns on investment. For policymakers, the data shows that prevention is dramatically cheaper than remediation once an invasive species becomes established.

title: Wild Pigs, Wild Costs: The Economic Consequences of Wild Pig Invasions in the United States
keyFinding: Wild pigs cause a minimum of $3.4 billion in annual damages to US agriculture, property, and natural resources — making them one of the costliest invasive species in the country.
impactSummary: Despite the scale of damage, wild pig management remains chronically underfunded relative to the costs they generate. This study makes the economic case for proactive population management before costs compound further — a direct example of where the economics of wildlife management and animal welfare intersect. Unmanaged wild pig populations cause both ecological destruction and significant harm to other animals and ecosystems.

title: Cost-Benefit Analysis as a Decision Tool for Effective Conservation Planning
keyFinding: Despite being standard practice in nearly every other field, cost-benefit analysis is rarely applied to wildlife conservation decisions — leaving conservation funding systematically underoptimized.
impactSummary: This peer-reviewed study demonstrates cost-benefit analysis applied to a wildlife corridor project in Tanzania, showing how economic tools can maximize conservation outcomes per dollar spent, address distributional equity, and inform community-based conservation. The implication for CAWE's audience is direct: conservation funding decisions made without economic analysis leave measurable value on the table and fail both animals and the communities that depend on them.

title: Economic Valuation of Wildlife Conservation
keyFinding: Economic valuations of wildlife have been used to set conservation priorities, assess co-benefits, and drive novel incentive schemes — but mainstream economics conceives of wildlife value too narrowly, missing institutional and equity dimensions.
impactSummary: This comprehensive review of wildlife valuation methods both makes the case for economic analysis in conservation and honestly identifies where that analysis falls short. For CAWE's audience this is essential reading: it shows how economic tools have successfully advanced conservation goals, while also flagging the risk of reducing complex ecological and community values to a single dollar figure. A rigorous, intellectually honest addition to the repository.

title: The Burden of Antimicrobial Resistance in Livestock: A Framework to Estimate Its Impact
keyFinding: Antimicrobial resistance in livestock has far-reaching social and economic consequences beyond the farm gate — including on human health and the environment — but standardized methods for quantifying this burden have not yet existed.
impactSummary: This peer-reviewed framework proposes standardized methods for quantifying the burden of antibiotic use and resistance in livestock — an essential tool for farm-level decision making and policy development. For CAWE, this is the missing link between farm animal welfare and public health economics: how the treatment of animals in agriculture becomes a cost borne by human healthcare systems. The livestock industry's routine antibiotic use is not just an animal welfare problem — it is a trillion-dollar public health liability.

title: FAO Report: Long-Term Economic Case for Tackling Antimicrobial Resistance in Livestock
authors: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO)
keyFinding: Antimicrobial growth promoters may offer short-term productivity gains, but cumulative global welfare losses from high antimicrobial resistance could reach $1.25 trillion by 2040 — far exceeding the $80 billion cost of phasing them out.
impactSummary: The livestock industry's short-term economic incentive to continue antibiotic use is directly at odds with its long-term economic survival. The FAO's scenario-based modelling makes this tradeoff quantifiable for the first time at a global scale. This is exactly the kind of misaligned incentive CAWE exists to make visible — where the economics of humane, welfare-forward farming align with long-term business resilience far better than current practices do.

title: Antimicrobials in Livestock Farming and Resistance: Public Health Implications
keyFinding: The accelerated spread of bacterial resistance reduces the effectiveness of antibiotic treatments, resulting in higher morbidity, mortality, and costs for livestock producers. It is expected that the majority of future global antimicrobial use will occur in animal production.
impactSummary: This peer-reviewed study connects farm-level antibiotic practices directly to the broader public health crisis of antimicrobial resistance — making clear that how we treat farm animals is inseparable from how resistant our medicine becomes. For producers, the economics are increasingly clear: antibiotic-dependent farming faces rising costs as resistance grows, while welfare-forward farming that reduces antibiotic dependence through better animal health management is positioned for long-term resilience.

title: The Rising Cost of Pet Care
keyFinding: Between 2021 and 2024, pet services including veterinary care increased at an average rate of 7.43% per year — with prices up nearly 25% since 2021, considerably outpacing overall inflation.
impactSummary: 50% of pet parents without pet insurance reported worrying about how to pay the vet bill if a pet gets sick. This piece translates Bureau of Labor Statistics data into plain language, making it ideal for general audiences seeking to understand why pet care has become a financial burden for millions of households. The compounding effect of sustained above-inflation price increases is eroding pet owners's ability to provide basic care — with direct consequences for animal welfare.

title: Study Shows Rising Pet Food, Veterinary Costs Top Challenges to Pet Ownership
keyFinding: 42% of pet owners cite pet food prices as a major challenge, while 39% point to veterinary expenses. These financial pressures are influencing pet ownership decisions at scale — deterring new adoptions and threatening long-term pet care stability.
impactSummary: The shift away from the stable spending patterns seen in 2021 is unlikely to reverse unless inflation stabilizes. For CAWE, this data connects the economics of pet ownership directly to shelter intake trends and the broader welfare of companion animals. When ownership becomes unaffordable, animals enter the shelter system — shifting costs from private households to public and nonprofit infrastructure that is already under strain.

title: America's Animal Shelters Are Overwhelmed. Pets and Staff Are at Breaking Point.
authors: Economic Hardship Reporting Project / The Guardian
keyFinding: After a record low of 5.5 million shelter intakes in 2020, animal intakes are increasing — with 6.5 million animals entering shelters in 2023, and only a little over 6 million leaving.
impactSummary: The cost of living crisis has direct consequences for animal welfare: as households face economic pressure, pets enter the shelter system at higher rates, stay longer, and face greater risk of euthanasia. Published jointly by the Economic Hardship Reporting Project and The Guardian, this piece makes the economic roots of the shelter crisis visible to a mainstream audience — connecting housing costs, inflation, and income instability directly to animal outcomes. Shelter staff burnout from performing euthanasia adds a human cost dimension rarely captured in policy discussions.
